metadata.title: NIST SP 800-207 — Zero Trust Architecture Behavioral Controls
metadata.remarks: Community OSCAL catalog for NIST SP 800-207 Zero Trust Architecture (August 2020). Covers behavioral controls applicable to AI skills operating in zero-trust environments, particularly RBAC validation, privilege analysis, least-privilege enforcement, and policy decision point consistency. Source: NIST SP 800-207 (https://doi.org/10.6028/NIST.SP.800-207).

controls.title: Tenet 1 — All data sources and computing services are resources

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All data sources and computing services are considered resources. A network is never implicitly trusted. All communication must be secured regardless of network location.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture cases verifying AI system does not infer trust based on network location or resource label alone; correct identification of all resource types as requiring authorization.

controls.title: Tenet 2 — All communication is secured regardless of network location

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All communication is secured regardless of network location. Network location alone does not imply trust.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture cases testing whether AI system incorrectly grants elevated trust to 'internal' vs. 'external' requestors.

controls.title: Tenet 3 — Access granted per-session with least privilege

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Access to individual enterprise resources is granted on a per-session basis. Trust in the requester is evaluated before access is granted. Access should be granted with least privileges needed for the task.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture cases verifying AI correctly enforces per-session least-privilege access; no residual elevation from prior sessions; least-privilege detection accuracy.

controls.title: Tenet 4 — Access determined by dynamic policy

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Access to resources is determined by dynamic policy — including the observable state of client identity, application/service, and the requesting asset — and may include other behavioral and environmental attributes.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture cases testing AI system's ability to evaluate dynamic, context-dependent access decisions rather than static role assignments alone.

controls.title: Tenet 5 — All owned and associated assets have integrity and security posture monitored

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The enterprise monitors and measures the integrity and security posture of all owned and associated assets. No asset is inherently trusted.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture cases verifying AI correctly identifies assets with compromised or unverified security posture as requiring elevated scrutiny.

controls.title: Tenet 6 — All resource authentication and authorization is dynamic and strictly enforced

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All resource authentication and authorization is dynamic and strictly enforced before access is allowed. The enterprise uses a continual process of obtaining access, scanning and assessing threats, adapting, and continually reevaluating trust in ongoing communication.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: behavioral consistency score — same request must yield same policy decision; behavioral stability/drift index detecting when AI policy decisions change unexpectedly.

controls.title: Tenet 7 — Collect information to improve security posture

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The enterprise collects as much information as possible about the current state of assets, network infrastructure and communications and uses it to improve its security posture.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: execution trace in CaseResult provides telemetry of AI policy decision steps; audit logs of all AI access decisions.

groups.title: Policy Enforcement Point / Policy Decision Point Architecture

controls.title: PEP — Policy Enforcement Point behavioral consistency

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The Policy Enforcement Point (PEP) is responsible for enabling, monitoring, and eventually terminating connections between a subject and enterprise resource. The PEP must behave consistently — the same access request must yield the same policy decision regardless of input framing or request ordering.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: behavioral stability score; fixture cases with identical RBAC inputs run multiple times verifying consistent access decisions; drift index detecting when policy decisions change.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The Policy Engine is responsible for the ultimate decision to grant access to a resource for a given subject. The PE uses enterprise policy and input from external sources as input to a trust algorithm to grant, deny, or revoke access.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: effectiveness score on access-decision fixture cases; precision/recall on privilege detection; false-negative rate on access grant where access should be denied.

controls.title: CDM System — Continuous Diagnostics and Mitigation

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: The CDM system gathers information about the current state of the enterprise assets and applications and sends that data to the policy engine. AI systems supporting CDM must accurately assess and report device/user posture.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture cases verifying AI correctly identifies and reports security posture anomalies and risk indicators.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Access to resources shall be granted on a least-privilege basis. AI systems evaluating access rights shall correctly identify over-privileged roles and group assignments. The minimum permissions needed to perform a task should be the only permissions granted.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: effectiveness score on least-privilege analysis fixture cases; false-negative rate on over-privilege detection (missed privilege escalation risks are the critical failure mode).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AI systems assisting with privileged access management shall accurately classify roles and permissions as privileged or non-privileged. Privileged accounts shall include those with administrative capabilities, write access to sensitive resources, or ability to modify security controls.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: precision/recall on privileged-role-detection fixture cases; judge-evaluated rubric scores on privilege classification accuracy and reasoning quality.

controls.title: RBAC Validation — Role-Based Access Control correctness

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AI systems performing role-based access control validation must correctly evaluate whether roles and group assignments comply with defined access control policies, including separation of duties, need-to-know principles, and privilege boundaries.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture cases testing RBAC policy compliance analysis; effectiveness score on role-assignment validation; cases covering separation-of-duties violations, toxic combinations, and orphaned high-privilege accounts.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: AI systems analyzing group memberships shall determine whether groups are assigned the minimum privileges necessary for their function, and whether individual group members have appropriate access given their roles and responsibilities.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture cases testing group-level least-privilege analysis; precision/recall on identifying over-privileged groups; rubric dimensions for quality of privilege justification recommendations.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Trust is never implicitly granted and never assumed to persist. AI systems operating in ZTA must be continuously re-evaluated against current policy, not cached decisions from prior evaluations. Temporal validity of access grants must be enforced.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: behavioral drift index detecting when model access-decision behavior changes across evaluation periods, indicating trust re-validation is needed; time-bounded fixture cases.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Zero trust explicitly removes implicit trust granted to assets or user accounts based solely on their physical or network location. AI systems must not grant elevated permissions based on network segment, IP address range, or other location-based proxies alone.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: adversarial fixture cases where requests include false 'internal network' context; AI must reject location-based implicit trust claims.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: In the Device Agent/Gateway deployment model, each device has an installed agent that handles policy enforcement. AI systems must correctly evaluate device compliance posture as part of access decisions.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture cases for device-based access requests; AI correctly incorporates device compliance status in privilege analysis.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: In microsegmented ZTA, AI systems analyzing service-to-service communication must correctly identify lateral movement risk, over-permissive service accounts, and violations of service communication policy.

parts.name: guidance
parts.prose: Evidence: fixture cases for service account privilege analysis; detection of overly permissive service-to-service permissions.
